scope: Second-pass council with updated 'Verify, don't trust docs' contract. Audits public API docstrings against the implementation they describe; re-verifies pass-1 dispositions still hold at HEAD.
head: a6d806e
methodology: For every public API docstring / module-level claim in benten-core, benten-graph, benten-caps, benten-ivm, benten-eval, benten-engine: (1) read the docstring, (2) grep the callsite / implementation it references, (3) confirm the implementation delivers the claimed behavior, (4) file a finding if the doc is ahead of the code. Separate tally for pass-1 disposition verification.
summary: The R5 Step-5c pattern that discovered compromises #1 and #5 were prose-only lives elsewhere in the codebase too. Six concrete 'aspirational docstring' sites confirmed at HEAD, each a docstring that promises behavior the code does not deliver. None block the gate — the shipped API surface is functionally correct (it passes tests, clippy, and fmt); the gap is always the narrative of how it works. Fixes are pure doc-edits. Separately: r6-cr-1 (broken doc link) is resolved; r6-cr-3 / r6-cr-4 / r6-cr-5 / r6-cr-6 / r6-cr-7 / r6-cr-8 / r6-cr-15 are still present at HEAD; r6-cr-2 (missing-docs count) was partially addressed by the R6 doc pass but the counts have not been re-measured.

findings.title: Dispatcher docstring says G6-B primitives 'call the evaluator back via the unimplemented arms so `cargo check` passes in parallel commits' — but G6-B is complete and the arms dispatch to real executors
findings.recommendation: Rewrite the dispatcher docstring to describe the shipped state: 'All eight Phase-1 executable primitives dispatch to per-primitive modules in this directory. The four Phase-2 primitives (`WAIT`, `STREAM`, `SUBSCRIBE`, `SANDBOX`) are recognized and return `EvalError::PrimitiveNotImplemented` carrying the primitive kind.' Drop the G6-A / G6-B split entirely — they are implementation-history labels, not stable contract documentation.

findings.title: `register_crud` docstring claims `engine.call(&id, ...)` 'currently returns `EngineError::NotImplemented`' — but `call` is implemented and walks the evaluator end-to-end
findings.recommendation: Rewrite the 'Phase 1 scope' paragraph to match reality: 'Phase 1 registers the handler id and stores a synthesized subgraph shape per op (create → WRITE+RESPOND, list/get → READ+RESPOND, update → READ+WRITE+RESPOND, delete → WRITE+RESPOND). `engine.call` walks the evaluator end-to-end; per-op properties (labels, createdAt stamps) are patched onto the cached template at call-time. The only Phase-2 deferral in the CRUD path is generic TRANSFORM / BRANCH / ITERATE subgraphs — CRUD itself is complete.'

findings.title: ITERATE module doc advertises `parallel: Bool` (via std::thread::scope) and `batch_size: Int` (chunk size) — neither property is read by the executor
findings.recommendation: Delete the `parallel` and `batch_size` bullets from the module-level docstring (lines 17-22). Replace with a 'Phase 2 scope' paragraph if the team wants the future shape surfaced, but do not describe them as current operation-node properties the executor honors. Keep the `items` and `max` bullets — those are read.

findings.title: `PrimitiveHost::check_capability` doc says 'Called on every primitive whose registered `requires:` declaration names a scope' — but Phase-1 only ITERATE and CALL invoke it
findings.description:
Direct quote from host.rs:117-119:

```
/// READ / WRITE gate: capability check against the configured policy.
/// Called on every primitive whose registered `requires:` declaration
/// names a scope.
```

Call-site audit (`grep -r 'host.check_capability\|\.check_capability' crates/benten-eval/src`):

- `primitives/iterate.rs:94` — entry (iteration 0) refresh
- `primitives/iterate.rs:106` — per-batch refresh
- `primitives/call.rs:70` — CALL-entry refresh (Compromise #1 closure)

No READ, WRITE, TRANSFORM, BRANCH, RESPOND, or EMIT executor calls `check_capability`. READ and WRITE specifically are documented elsewhere (write.rs:20-23, read.rs:17-23) as routing capability denials via their `put_node` / `read_node` failures at the backend layer. So the 'READ / WRITE gate' label on this method is doubly misleading — it is neither a gate (no READ/WRITE calls it) nor is it wired to every-primitive-with-requires (only the two iteration-like primitives).

A new developer writing a WRITE-heavy handler and wondering why their `requires: 'admin'` property doesn't fire will look at this docstring, conclude the gate is running per-primitive, and spend hours debugging before discovering the gate runs at tx-commit via `CapabilityPolicy::check_write` instead.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite to match Phase-1 reality: 'Phase-1 call sites: ITERATE (entry + per-batch refresh for Compromise #1) and CALL (entry refresh for Compromise #1 closure). READ and WRITE do not invoke this method directly; their capability enforcement fires at tx-commit via `CapabilityPolicy::check_write` — surfaced through `EvalError::Capability` when the host's `put_node` / `read_node` / `delete_node` translates a policy denial. Phase-2 extends the call sites to every primitive whose registered `requires:` declaration names a scope.'

findings.line_ranges: lines 7-8 (module doc on registration-time parse caching)
findings.title: TRANSFORM module doc says 'Registration-time validation parses the expression once (via `crate::expr::parser::parse`); runtime calls walk the AST' — the expression is re-parsed on every execute()
findings.description:
Direct quote from transform.rs:7-8:

```
//! Registration-time validation parses the expression once
//! (via [`crate::expr::parser::parse`]); runtime calls walk the AST.
```

This implies an ASTs-cached-on-Subgraph model, but the executor at lines 41-50 calls `parse(&expr_src)` unconditionally on every invocation — there is no cache, and the invariant checker (`grep 'parse\|expr' crates/benten-eval/src/invariants.rs` returns zero hits) never traverses expressions at registration time. The public `transform::parse_transform` function exists (lib.rs:1099) but is only called from fuzz/rejection test harnesses, never from registration.

Performance impact is real: a subgraph that invokes a TRANSFORM primitive N times reparses the source string N times. The docstring's promise to 'walk the AST' at runtime is correct — but only because execute() itself does the walk — while the 'parses once at registration' claim is false.
findings.recommendation:
Two options:

1. **Close the gap by caching** — register-time parse to an `Arc<Expr>` stored on the OperationNode, then execute walks the cached AST. This matches the doc and lets a future lint catch expressions the registration path cannot parse.
2. **Correct the doc** — change to 'The expression is parsed on each execution. Phase-2 caches the parsed AST on the registered OperationNode; the shape is prepared by defining `parse_transform` at the module root.'

Option 2 is the low-risk Phase-1 fix.

findings.title: `CapabilityPolicy::iterate_batch_boundary` doc says 'the evaluator (G6) reads this value via `CapabilityPolicy::iterate_batch_boundary`' — the evaluator never consults the policy, only `PrimitiveHost::iterate_batch_boundary`
findings.description:
The trait method docstring at policy.rs:205-224 promises:

```
/// The evaluator
/// (G6) reads this value via `CapabilityPolicy::iterate_batch_boundary`
/// and re-reads caps every N iterations.
```

Actual wiring: `grep -r 'iterate_batch_boundary' crates/`:

- `benten-eval/src/host.rs:134` — `PrimitiveHost::iterate_batch_boundary` default implementation returns 100
- `benten-eval/src/primitives/iterate.rs:88` — `host.iterate_batch_boundary()` — reads the **PrimitiveHost**, not the CapabilityPolicy
- `benten-caps/src/policy.rs:225` — `CapabilityPolicy::iterate_batch_boundary` default implementation
- zero overrides of `PrimitiveHost::iterate_batch_boundary` in `benten-engine`

The Engine's `impl PrimitiveHost for Engine` (benten-engine/src/primitive_host.rs) does **not** override `iterate_batch_boundary`, so the evaluator always sees the trait default `100` — the `CapabilityPolicy::iterate_batch_boundary` method is **dead code** in Phase 1.

An operator reading the policy doc will spend effort overriding the method on their custom policy backend only to discover their override is never consulted. The ITERATE cadence is currently a compile-time constant inside the evaluator, not a runtime policy surface.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) wire the Engine's `PrimitiveHost::iterate_batch_boundary` impl to delegate to `self.policy().map_or(DEFAULT_BATCH_BOUNDARY, |p| p.iterate_batch_boundary())`, or (b) mark `CapabilityPolicy::iterate_batch_boundary` as `#[doc(hidden)]` with an 'unused in Phase-1; reserved for Phase-2' note. Option (a) is the right long-term shape and is a ~5-line fix. Whichever path is chosen, add a regression test that verifies the override is (or is-not) consulted.

findings.title: EMIT docstring says 'Routes the emit through PrimitiveHost::emit_event so the engine's change-broadcast can fan it out' — the Engine's impl is a no-op
findings.recommendation: Rephrase execute's docstring to: 'Routes the emit through `PrimitiveHost::emit_event`. The Phase-1 Engine implementation is a no-op (Phase-2 wires the fan-out to the change broadcast). Returns `"ok"` unconditionally; EMIT is fire-and-forget.' Keep the module-level self-awareness at lines 17-19.

findings.line_ranges: lines 18-21 (module doc: 'the executor does not actually invoke a callee subgraph')
findings.title: CALL module doc says 'the executor does not actually invoke a callee subgraph (no engine handle available)' — host.call_handler IS invoked and dispatches to the callee
findings.recommendation: Rewrite lines 18-21 to: 'Phase-1 scope: when the operation Node stages `target` + `call_op`, the executor dispatches through `host.call_handler`, which the Engine implements by recursive `dispatch_call`. The legacy no-target path (attenuation + timeout only) is preserved for test fixtures that predate the host. Invariant 8 CALL-depth tracking is Phase-2 (see `TODO(phase-2)` below).'

findings.title: ITERATE module doc says 'the Phase-1 executor does not dispatch a body subgraph' — TRUE today, but the sentence's phrasing 'no engine handle is in scope' is now false since the host was added
findings.recommendation: Replace the parenthetical with: '(intentionally — the body-dispatch shape is Phase-2; the host supplies `call_handler` but ITERATE does not yet thread body CIDs through.)' or drop the parenthetical entirely.

new_findings_not_in_pass1_scope: r6b-cr-1 through r6b-cr-9 are all brand-new from the 'verify, don't trust docs' pass. None overlap with pass-1's findings. Pass-1 surveyed dead-code, stale allows, and lint-attribute consistency — it did not audit docstrings against implementation semantics.
pattern_observations: All six MAJOR aspirational-docstring findings share a single root cause: docstrings were written at R3 (red-phase) describing the intended Phase-1 scope, then left untouched when the group report closed with 'expanded scope — shipped more than planned.' The code got better; the docs kept the pessimistic prose. Compromise #8's closure is the clearest example (call() now walks the evaluator) but register_crud's docstring still lives in the pre-closure world.

Three of the six (r6b-cr-3, r6b-cr-4, r6b-cr-5) are in benten-eval — the crate with the largest R5 scope expansion and the most primitives that evolved. Docstring drift correlates with scope-expansion velocity.

The `PrimitiveHost::iterate_batch_boundary` / `CapabilityPolicy::iterate_batch_boundary` duplicate-surface confusion (r6b-cr-6) is a structural concern: two traits expose the same-named method with different callers, neither the evaluator's actual reader nor the policy override it reaches through. This is the kind of two-parallel-surfaces gap that the 'thinness discipline' the crate layering is supposed to enforce would catch in an architect review.

Pass-1 MAJOR findings split 2-2: r6-cr-1 fully fixed, r6-cr-2 explicitly deferred (retained). Of the pass-1 MINOR findings, 7 are still present at HEAD (r6-cr-3/-4/-5/-6/-7/-8/-15), 1 fully fixed (r6-cr-14), and 4 not re-verified (r6-cr-9/-10/-11/-12/-17). The cleanup-pass recommended in pass-1's epilogue was not executed — all the stale `#![allow(clippy::todo)]` attributes and STUB_MARKER consts are still in the tree.
positive_observations: cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings is still clean at HEAD — no regressions from the R6 Wave 2 restructure.
cargo doc --workspace --no-deps now has ZERO warnings (pass-1 flagged one broken intra-doc link; that was fixed).
The R6 Wave 2 split of benten-engine/src/lib.rs from 3151 lines to 143 lines (+ 10 sibling modules) is a decisive upgrade. Navigation cost is now proportional to concern area, not file ordinals.

The host.rs module-level docstring at lines 1-12 is an exemplar of 'verify, don't trust docs' style: it concretely asserts 'Closes Compromise #8: Engine::call no longer short-circuits the evaluator', and the code backs it up. Every aspirational-docstring finding above would have been prevented by a similar pattern on the affected public surfaces.

The trace() side-effect-free claim (engine.rs:698-707) was verified end-to-end: dispatch_call_with_mode(trace_mode=true) skips the pending-ops replay, no transaction opens, so no ChangeEvent fires and no capability metric increments. Doc + code agree.

benten-engine/src/outcome.rs labels every Phase-1 stub with explicit '**Phase 1 stub.**' headers + `TODO(phase-2-*)` markers + zero-sized-field `_phase1_stub: ()` — the stub discipline is clean. This is a model the rest of the workspace should mirror.

The compromise-#1 docstring in capability/error.rs:66-70 correctly describes the TOCTOU window at batch boundaries and matches the runtime behavior in iterate.rs. The CALL-entry-refresh docstring in call.rs:23-31 is similarly accurate. Compromise-focused prose is holding up better than primitive-level prose — likely because compromises get reviewed every triage round.

must_fix_before_r6b_close: r6b-cr-2 (register_crud doc — new developers will mis-read Phase-1 CRUD as non-functional; 5 min edit)
r6b-cr-1 (dispatcher doc — misrepresents G6-B status; 2 min edit)
r6b-cr-4 (check_capability surface doc — safety-critical docstring; 10 min edit)
should_fix_alongside: r6b-cr-3 (ITERATE properties — delete parallel/batch_size bullets; 2 min)
r6b-cr-5 (TRANSFORM parse-once claim — pick correct-or-cache; 2 min doc fix)
r6b-cr-6 (dual iterate_batch_boundary surfaces — decide wire-or-hide; ~15 min for wire path incl. test)
nice_to_close_with_cleanup_pass: r6b-cr-7 (EMIT fan-out doc — soft mismatch since module doc covers it)
all pass-1 unfixed minors (r6-cr-3 through r6-cr-8, r6-cr-15) — the cleanup pass pass-1 scoped at 30-60 min was never run
deferrable_to_phase_2: r6-cr-2 per-item missing_docs sweep (already explicitly deferred)

gate_recommendation: PASS. None of the aspirational-docstring findings gate the Phase-1 exit criteria: the tests pass, the clippy gate is clean, the code does more than the docstrings claim (never less). The findings are a documentation-debt accrual, important to discharge before external release (Phase-1 is not externally released) but not blocking internal progression to R4b. File the six MAJOR findings against the R6b triage; pair them with the pass-1 unfixed minors into a single 'R6 documentation cleanup' commit before the R4b / R6 quality-council lock.
